item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, I'm curious about the other two occasions where those events occurred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have never read an SCP so…SCP. Like this is what the site is all about. It's so bizarre, so esoteric, so…interesting and REALLY MESSED UP, but beautiful in its own way. Thank you.

+1
Thank you :)

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I’m pretty sure someone stole your idea and turned it into a show called thirsty just an FYI if you didn’t already know about it. Also I love your story it’s so creative and unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
okay, the 049 x Leslie crackship makes a little more sense now.

still, i can't believe i let myself get emotionally invested in a romance starring a cyborg mosquito. also, i have a soft spot for the Foundation doing nice things for civilians affected by scips, so that ending really got to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is some real bee movie shit right here take my +1 and leave

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just out of curiosity, did you get ay inspiration from Zeus in Greek mythology? I believe he had a relationship with a goddess that turned into a mosquito, and he also bore Dionysus in his thigh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was the most emotional file I’ve Ever read😭. I don’t normally read anything romantic, but that was so beautiful *blows nose in handkerchief*. They loved each other so much, it was just like the movie they were watching! The ending is perfect, The Foundation actually did the right thing! I can just imagine whoever was in charge of that mission was crying at the end. +1! +1 all the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least he got to keep the kids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was this inspired in any way by that Asterix story with the messenger fly?

Great SCP regardless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This dips it's toe in greentext territory, but god, it's so heart-wrenching.

Nice read. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great SCP, but I have a couple suggestions. First, towards the end, the line "instance of an instance" could be worded differently so that it flows better. Second, I would add something in procedures about the monitoring of the -A instances, though perhaps under a level 3 tab to maintain the page's integrity.
Either way, great SCP, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saddest SCP…
;(

My first +200!!!

My girl has come so far in this world

Thank you to everyone who supported her!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: HOLY CRAP, THAT WAS CUTE AS ALL HECK!!!! BUT THEN IT GOT SAD AS ALL HECK!!! I never thought I'd ever feel bad for a mosquito!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …the kids though??? I kinda want to see what they'll be like when they grow up. Will they have anomalous abilities??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best character pieces on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought that mosquito was getting an accidental slap.

Everything went so much better than I could have guessed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Typo fixed; thanks for notifying staff!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Page is locked, so I am requesting a typo fix. In 'Video Log 3774-14' at the second paragraph / when the subject first talks they say, 'you better play close attention,' saying 'play' rather than 'pay.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I’m glad that even the footnotes don’t go unnoticed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the best use of footnotes I've seen in a comic(ish) format!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More importantly, why are you on the SCP Wiki in the club right now?

(Thank you for liking it though)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why tf am i crying in the club right now?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The heart of this SCP is to make an anomaly tragic without the ending having to be the elimination of all things involving the anomaly. I’ve seen plenty of anomalies that build up a reason to cheer for the anomaly only to have them be destroyed for some Foundation-y reason, and I wanted to spin that on its head and have a happy ending, but also use the Foundation’s motives to achieve this. Specifically, altering memories, not erasing them, and letting Merle keep the kids, although on 24/7 monitoring.

It’s basically my take on a tragic romance story mixed with the Foundation aspect, and I’m sorry that it didn’t land for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not doing much for me.

The base concept is completely implausible: we're not given any particular reason the Foundation would break character to create 60's-spy-parody level supertech, just told that it happened. (I canon a high tech Foundation, but I still need some build-up if I'm supposed to buy that.)

From that barely set up premise, you launch into a bunch of logs that did have me feeling sympathy for the characters, but they were also way too long and had a lot of very corny dialogue. Then you end with a fairly well-executed weird SCP twist, but it didn't land as hard because the setup didn't give me anyexpectations to violate.

I could get behind an SCP that's nonsensical and incongruous for style reasons, or an SCP with a genre twist it has invited me into, but this doesn't feel intentional. It's also ok to have one that's just to produce a surreal mental image, e.g. a mosquito in love, but if that's the plan don't make me sit through this many logs for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That’s the idea m8 👉🏻👉🏻

Thank you for the vote though! I’m glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
makin me feel bad about a damn mosquito, smh
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can't have success without the succ

And thank you very much for the up vote! I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Insert joke about Merle "getting the suck" here.

In all serious though, this was really well-written. I strongly enjoyed the interactions between the two main characters. The dialogue made me legitimately care about their relationship even if it was a tad clunky at times (I really shouldn't be talking because I can't write dialogue for shit).

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s an honor to be compared to one of my favorite skips, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With this and SCP-1470, we only need one more to make a "dead bug that made me want to cry" trilogy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting theory, and one I didn’t consider writing it, but certainly a fun idea.

I’m glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another commenter pointed out how stupid it was letting these things vocalize.

I choose to headcanon that the reason the things go haywire when given the ability to speak is the same reason they were given the ability to speak at all: some internal force in the Foundation, or maybe some related anomaly, tampered with the project's road map long before the speech issues arose (and is the reason that speech was even part of the program).

Aside from that, what the fuck did I just read anyway +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I’m glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh this is wonderful. It drew me in with crazy biotech mosquito spies, and featured messages about romance, mortality, the ethics of creating intelligences… excellent, excellent, excellent

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I really do appreciate the input

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, Shaggydredlocks clearly stated what I had in mind.. And the final log didn't fully express all the emotions as expected. I enjoyed the conversations a lot, though. Laughed when Leslie said "useless happy relationships".

Good luck with your future articles anyway!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, okay. Thank you for clarifying! Hopefully if I ever do video logs again, I'll take more advantage of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what they're talking about - it's pretty clear in retrospect: basically, they're saying that you're not making full use of the format. You're providing 'video' logs, but with almost no discernible action - so they fall into a "talking heads" scenario where there's very little action tags/stage direction to show how the scenes play out.

It's justified in the third and (to an extent) final logs, where a) the character is just sitting around and b) the camera is disabled, but it's really apparent throughout the rest (excluding the first half of the first log).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don’t worry about your English, it was perfect.

Also, I don’t understand what you mean by it doesn’t allow you to participate in the skip. Could you explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, downvoted this one.. I am only one of the few people that are not satisfied with the article, so please do not let my personal opinion bother you…And sorry for my poor English.

I understand that, theoretically, the only possible record of the SCP is video record. Still, as an author, one might perhaps adjust the details to determine the best method to present a story. In my perspective of view, a love story like this does not work well with video logs. As a reader, I feel like I am watching a movie that rarely changes its camera angle, without actually "participating" in the story. The article contains mostly conversations and I feel like I am just watching them talking..

And the story is.. not the best love story among SCP articles. The characters and the story itself is not lifelike enough in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m glad my writing was able to appeal to that, then!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm such a fucking romantic

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd be interested if you could send a link to those mosquito soap operas you mentioned?

For research, of course

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is why I don't watch soap operas…
And Mosquito ones at that…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A whole towel? I’m honored!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take my +1, I need to find a crying towel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the Duck?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s the fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I see it, there were thousands of instances to be collected, and I figured it would just take a while to retrieve them. However, since the Foundation was curious to see the result of these instances entering a romantic relationship, they let the three instances that were able to successfully court a subject live their full lives without retrieval. They wouldn’t know that there would be adverse effects until the end.

Aside from that, I’m glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although this does raise a lot of questions and seems like a very flawed project from the Foundation's side, it's weirdly heartwarming and heartbreaking enough for me to upvote. Despite those flaws I quite enjoyed it. One thing in particular does confuse me though, how exactly did they get the videos? If the mosquito was broadcasting it I'd imagine they'd catch on to the error right away, and if it remained in one place for several days that'd also seem like a cause for sooner investigation than waiting for several days after it should be dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's my goal and I'm so glad that it came through. Thank you, Cloud!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the actual hell am I even feeling right now? This story is so freaking good. You managed to make me like, care and cry over these characters. (A mosquito at that)

It's both a tragic, funny, and a heartwarming story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, sure. Just trying to make the ending seem relatively rewarding

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fine details would just make it drag, I feel. I'd just change their methods: visual, physical, and imaging tech to determine they're human for all intents and purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s a fair point. Should I just change that part of the ending or would it be better suited to reword it to include more fine details about -A’s anatomy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a final nitpick; it probably would've been more heartwarming had they not excised and cruelly terminated one of the fetuses, to come to a simple conclusion that could have been reached via non-intrusive/destructive methods. It was hard to really be sold on a happy ending when one of the children were killed so the SCPF could throw their hands up and say 'whelp, looks human enough' (to be more clear, if you're not going to get into explicit detail of their biology, why resort to something so extreme to begin with?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, I’m glad I was able to do that for you! I appreciate the upvote!
